item.title: Taliban says it's open to talks after Pakistan bombs Kabul and other Afghan towns
item.description: As the hostilities between Pakistan and Afghanistan are increasing, the Taliban officials say they "want to resolve this matter through dialogue".

item.title: As the threat of war heightens distress, help may be just a phone call away
item.description: A national hotline offers multilingual support for refugees who've fled war and violence. As global conflict continues, demand is soaring.

item.title: INTERVIEW: How to cope with rising power prices
item.description: A new survey by financial comparison website CanStar has found that many Australian households are currently worried or have concerns about future energy bill payments as federal rebates run out this year. This is in addition to an underlying price increase in household electricity bills. Over the last month, they've risen by approximately 4.5% according to the Australian Bureau of Statistics. CanStar's Data Insights Director, Sally Tindall spoke to SBS's Cameron Carr about what the latest data is showing.

item.title: A new community-led initiative offers support to vulnerable asylum seekers
item.description: Despite record numbers of displaced people worldwide, resettlement options are facing a critical decline - global impacts including shrinking legal pathways, slashing of admission numbers and funding cuts to agencies. But for the first time, a new community-led initiative will support LGBTIQ+ refugees to apply for Australia’s humanitarian program - offering dedicated support for priority processing.

item.title: Wong warns Australians in Middle East of 'difficult days ahead' as flights cancelled
item.description: Some believe that Middle East airspace could be "shut for quite some time" amid the latest conflict between the US, Israel and Iran.

item.title: In pictures: Colour, protest and pride at this year's Sydney Mardi Gras
item.description: Chants of both pride and protest echoed around Sydney as thousands of people marched along Oxford Street for the annual Mardi Gras parade.

item.title: In pictures: Colour, protest and pride at this year's Sydney Mardi Gras
item.description: Chants of both pride and protest echoed around Sydney as thousands of people marched along Oxford Street for the annual Mardi Gras parade.

item.title: Were the Israeli-US strikes on Iran legal under international law? These experts say no
item.description: The government's refusal to publicly assess the legality of the strikes risks "trashing" international law, an expert says.

item.title: 'We're grounded': Australian family's regret over passports trapped in 'nightmare' limbo
item.description: Gemma has had to send physical passports, birth certificates and a marriage licence overseas as part of new entry laws.

item.title: Who is leading Iran now — and how will a new supreme leader be chosen?
item.description: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei is dead, leaving Iran at war and without its most powerful figure. Here's how the system will choose his successor.

item.title: Will a 'survival pact' in Iran keep the regime alive after Khamenei?
item.description: The death of Iran's supreme leader Ali Khamenei raises serious questions about the future of the regime and its clerical rulers.

item.title: Trump says the US strikes on Iran could last up to four weeks | SBS News in Easy English 2 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: When Australians can get the best view of the 'eerie' blood moon
item.description: A rare 'blood moon' will be visible over Australia soon. Here's when to look up.

item.title: $1 a litre more: Three oil price scenarios and how they'd hit Australians
item.description: Australians' wallets will be hit harder the longer the war goes on — and not just at the pump, economists have warned.

item.title: Iran's 'weaknesses' have long been clear. So why a major escalation?
item.description: These are the factors that may have influenced the United States and Israel's deadly attack against Iran.

item.title: 'Change has a price': Iranian eyewitnesses speak from the shadows of war
item.description: Messages received by SBS News capture the mood inside Iran as some rejoice over the killing of its supreme leader, while others mourn.

item.title: Death toll from attack in South Sudan rises to 169, including 90 civilians
item.description: An attack by a group of unidentified men has resulted in the loss of 169 lives in South Sudan, a death toll that could rise further.

item.title: Israel strikes in Lebanon turn deadly as Iran conflict widens
item.description: At least 52 people have been killed and more than 150 injured in Lebanon, as Israel targets operations of the Iran-allied militant group Hezbollah, after it launched missiles and drones toward Israel.

item.title: Iran war impacts petrol prices | SBS News in Easy English 3 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Australia's new $2,300 visa price increase and more key changes
item.description: Besides doubling graduate visa application fees, the federal government has quietly introduced two other key changes.

item.title: Why some in Australia are mourning Iran's Ayatollah Khamenei, as criticism mounts
item.description: While for many Khamenei was the "embodiment of a repressive regime", one expert explains the religious reasons some are mourning.

item.title: How could a war in the Middle East impact petrol prices?
item.description: Hear RBA Governor Michele Bullock detail how the Middle East tensions and hit to oil supply may impact inflation and what it means for interest rates; SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Cameron Gleeson from Betashares to find out what it means for sharemarkets; plus Life360 CEO Lauren Antonoff talks through her company's results and AI future.

item.title: Military action continues in the Middle East | SBS News in Easy English 4 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Rain-battered areas now face cyclone warning in Queensland's north
item.description: Australia's rain-battered far north is preparing to batten down the hatches again as a possible tropical cyclone looks set to cross the coast near Cairns in the coming days.

item.title: Matildas coach urges 'human compassion' for Iran football team amid striker's tears
item.description: Iranian striker Sara Didar fought back tears at a press conference ahead Thursday's Women's Asian Cup match against the Matildas.

item.title: Australian investors dump $63bn as Middle-East war stokes global inflation concerns
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Harry Murphy Cruise from Oxford Economics about the impact of the Middle East war on the Australian economy, even as GDP expands at its fastest rate in almost three years. Plus Niv Dagan from Peak Asset Management on the day's sharemarket news including the market's reaction to ARN Media's decision to pull the Kyle and Jackie O show.

item.title: Inmate turned advocate wants more support for women on the outside
item.description: A First Nations advocate and activist for incarcerated women says the social contract between the Australian government and the community is broken. Tahlia Isaac, who founded 'Project:herSelf', says authorities are more interested in punishment than providing the conditions to keep people safe. She says the system needs to change and the government needs to start listening to women who have real insight into the cycle of incarceration.

item.title: $2,850 and major flight doubts: Australian stranded in Saudi Arabia's travel nightmare
item.description: Many Muslims who have travelled to Saudi Arabia for Ramadan now find themselves unable to leave, with flights largely grounded.

item.title: From political oblivion to poll surge: Inside Pauline Hanson's comeback
item.description: Accusations of electoral fraud, persistent immigration rhetoric and a distinct orange tinge.

item.title: European leaders are voice concerns over war against Iran | SBS News in Easy English 5 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Iran’s regime on the brink? US-Israel strikes, oil shock fears & the battle for what comes next
item.description: With the Supreme Leader killed, Iran lashes out. Will the Middle East choose sides as oil prices skyrocket and travel chaos sees thousands stranded. Plus, who could be next in line in Iran, could the US strikes encourage others into action and proof that sometimes the best defence is a very loud, slightly unhinged cuddly toy.

item.title: Why airfares could rise in coming years & US data reignites rate cut hopes
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Robert Talevski from Activam about the day's market action while Stephanie Youseff discusses the ACCC's latest airports report which warns high levels of investment will be passed onto airlines which may increase airfares as a result.

item.title: 'We were already concerned': Alarm over Iranian broadcast labelling football team 'traitors'
item.description: Concerns about the safety of the Iranian team have been raised as sources visiting a hotel says they're treated like "prisoners" on tour.

item.title: 'Euro summer' in question as experts caution Australians over travel plans
item.description: Australians may need to "seriously reconsider" travel plans in the coming months.

item.title: Donald Trump says US wants to be involved in choosing Iran's next leader as conflict spreads
item.description: Donald Trump insists he has a say in picking Iran's next supreme leader, as the conflict reverberates throughout the region.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Indigenous Australians Minister on the parliamentary inquiry into racism
item.description: A federal parliamentary inquiry has opened into racism in Australia, with advocates calling for the probe to be not 'another exercise in diagnosis'. The Australian Human Rights Commission says the government already knows what needs to change, and the inquiry must turn that knowledge into action, with timelines and accountability. Meanwhile the federal Minister for Indigenous Australians Malandirri McCarthy is encouraging big social media platforms to make submissions to the inquiry. She's told NITV some Indigenous people are feeling scared after a string of reports of racist attacks in public. She's talking here to NITV's John Paul Janke

item.title: Israel's army to expand its area of control in southern Lebanon |SBS News in Easy English 6 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: ASX’s worst day since Liberation Day; oil price shock
item.description: The S&P/ASX 200 has recorded its worst weekly drop since Donald Trump’s so-called Liberation Day tariffs shocked global markets in April last year. That’s as investors weigh the prospect of a drawn-out war in the Middle East, with the rising oil price prompting further inflation concerns. Meanwhile, the US Pentagon has formally labelled AI company Anthropic a supply chain risk, escalating its dispute over AI safeguards. For more, Stephanie Youssef spoke with capital.com senior market analyst Kyle Rodda.

item.title: 'As long as it takes': Experts predict possible outcomes of war in the Middle East
item.description: As the War in the Middle East reaches the one week mark, experts speculate on how the crisis could play out.

item.title: Fears for low income families during Ramadan, as Middle East conflict threatens to send food prices higher
item.description: Families in Australia are bracing for a cost-of-living hit, as energy prices soar. Charities are increasing gifts of free food during Ramadan, to help those already struggling. SBS Arabic takes a look at how one group is trying to help.

item.title: The cost of war: How the Middle East conflict could worsen food insecurity in Australia
item.description: Long queues, scarce groceries and rising fuel bills — Australia's food insecurity may worsen, experts warn, as war in the Middle East threatens to drive up prices.

item.title: Russia returns to Paralympic Games, 16 countries boycott opening ceremony
item.description: Ukraine said boycotting the Paralympic Winter Games' opening ceremony was "a principled decision".

item.title: 'People confuse China's policy': Behind Russian, Chinese view on war in the Middle East
item.description: Russia, China and Iran have had close relationships for decades, but Beijing and Moscow have yet offered any direct support for Tehran.

item.title: He was shot by police during an uprising. His pain tells a bigger story
item.description: Shantanu set out to change Nepal. Instead, the gen Z protests changed him. Nepal will soon have a new government — but will it deliver real change?

item.title: It's International Women's Day. Are women any safer - and what else can be done for those fleeing violence?
item.description: A warning - the following story contains elements that may distress some people. In 2025, New South Wales alone recorded the highest number of women murdered in domestic violence situations. Advocates say it's not a one-off situation - and that women's safety remains an ongoing concern.

item.title: What's causing the Greek yoghurt shortage in Australian supermarkets?
item.description: Many shops are running low on Greek yoghurt. Has tzatziki suddenly become wildly popular, or is it the result of online trends?

item.title: SBS exclusive interview with ambassadors to Australia Fiona Flood, Beate Grzeski and Betty Pavelich
item.description: Today is International Women's Day, with the theme of 'Balancing the Scales' which calls for accelerated progress toward gender equity. SBS World News Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson has spoken with Irish Ambassador Fiona Flood, German Ambassador Beate Grzeski, and Croatian Ambassador Betty Pavelich on their experiences as senior diplomats. The conversation covers a range of important issues, including gender equality in world diplomacy, the conflict with Iran, and the struggles women have in getting ahead in a traditionally man's world of diplomacy.

item.title: When Janelle retired, she never guessed periods would be her new passion
item.description: Crafter Janelle Dodd has built a community to educate and help people around the world with menstruation.

item.title: Government urged to let Iranian women's football team stay in Australia until safety is assured
item.description: Several community and civil society groups have sent the home affairs minister a letter expressing "grave concerns" for the team.

item.title: 'We've reached a breaking point': Is it time to tax older Australians more?
item.description: Some Australians are richer than ever, while younger cohorts are struggling. Experts say it's time for change.

item.title: Who is Mojtaba Khamenei, Iran's new supreme leader?
item.description: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's son Mojtaba has succeeded his late father as supreme leader, in a sign that hardliners are still firmly in charge.

item.title: 'A disturbing picture': Australia's deadliest cancer is no longer lung cancer
item.description: A fast-growing group of cancers has quietly surpassed lung cancer as Australia's biggest killer, according to a new analysis.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 9 March 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for those with disability and those learning English

item.title: Flood alerts in Queensland and Northern Territory as residents evacuated, urged to seek high ground
item.description: Most of Queensland is on alert for flooding as a tropical low that hammered the far north continues across the southeast.

item.title: 'Maximum damage': Iran's tactics in the face of 'overwhelming' US force
item.description: Iran's retaliatory strikes reflect a strategy of survival, not victory, experts say, as war in the Middle East deepens.

item.title: How high could petrol prices rise? Eight ways to stretch your tank
item.description: The average price of unleaded petrol soared past $2 a litre in many parts of the country. So how can drivers save?

item.title: Australia's 'bare cupboard': What it can — and can't — offer the Gulf
item.description: As Gulf states face Iranian retaliatory strikes, defence experts detail the limited assets Australia could send — and gaps it can't fill.

item.title: 'Joy' and 'relief' for Iranian women's soccer team offered new life in Australia
item.description: Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke has offered the entire Iranian women's football team humanitarian visas, saying they're welcome to stay.

item.title: Sonja asked high schoolers to debate about tradwives — and received international backlash
item.description: In Australia, are terms such as "free speech" and "cultural safety" now used in place of constructive, respectful debate?

item.title: Regional markets smashed as oil surges over US $100
item.description: Nearly $90 billion dollars has been wiped from the S&P/ASX 200, as war in Iran sent oil prices skyrocketing past US$100 a barrel for the first time since Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022; The rising oil price sparks panic buying and reports of fuel rationing at some petrol stations in Australia.

item.title: PM says Australia will deploy RAAF plane and missiles to Gulf to protect civilians
item.description: The government has responded to a request for assistance by the United Arab Emirates, deploying a high-tech spy plane to the Middle East.

item.title: SBS News In Easy English 10 March 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for those learning English and those with disability

item.title: 'I've had enough': David Littleproud resigns as National Party leader
item.description: Nationals expected to select a new leader in coming days after David Littleproud quit the party's top job, saying he was "buggered".

item.title: Australia sends surveillance aircraft and troops to Middle East
item.description: Australia is deploying an E7A Wedgetail surveillance aircraft and 85 personnel to the United Arab Emirates to bolster regional air defences. The government is maintaining the mission is strictly defensive, while the Greens warn it risks entangling Australia in an illegal conflict.

item.title: Airfares to rise as much as 30% and ASX makes partial recovery
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Cirium Aviation Analyst Ellis Taylor who says airfares may rise as much as 30 per cent as jet fuel prices soar amid the Middle-East war; and Todd Hoare from LGT Wealth Management Australia Management on the markets partial recovery following yesterday's $90bn sell-off.

item.title: 'She can't make a decision': Desperate plea amid fears Iranian players are being coerced
item.description: After five players were granted asylum, concerns are mounting over whether the remaining team members can safely seek protection.

item.title: Bundaberg residents told to evacuate as floods trigger memories of 2010 disaster
item.description: Thousands of residents have been told to evacuate amid fears rising floodwaters could reach levels not seen in the area since 2010.

item.title: Scouring the countryside for 600L of fuel: Farmer Rob has 'never seen' anything like this
item.description: Farmers warn supply disruptions could threaten Australia's food production.

item.title: 15 years on, is the world better equipped to prevent a nuclear accident similar to Fukushima?
item.description: It's been 15 years since one of the worst natural disasters in history. The 2011 East Japan Earthquake and tsunami killed more than 19,000 people and displaced hundreds of thousands of people, especially those forced to evacuate following the subsequent Fukushima nuclear accident. So what lessons can be learnt from this disaster and nuclear accident in a climate of increasing global turmoil?

item.title: Matt Canavan calls for 'hyper Australia' with 'more babies' as he leads National Party
item.description: The party chose its new leader in a 45-minute meeting, following David Littleproud's surprise resignation on Tuesday.

item.title: Tony Burke says an Iranian football player has reversed her asylum decision
item.description: The home affairs minister said the federal government respected the decision she had made.

item.title: "We support you": Iranian women's football team members take up visa offer amid fears over return
item.description: The Australian government has granted humanitarian visas to seven members of the Iranian National women's football team to stay in Australia. Amid relief from the supporters within the community and fears for the remaining players, critics say the government's proposed visa legislation will leave other visa holders in the region stranded.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 11 March 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for those learning English and those with disability

item.title: Two more of the Iranian football party defect before team leaves Australia
'I think our country needs to be saved': Nationals elect Matt Canavan as new leader
item.description: Queensland Senator Matt Canavan has been elected leader of the Nationals following David Littleproud's resignation, appointing Darren Chester as his deputy. The conservative leader aims to challenge net-zero targets, address fuel crises, and advocate for child care reform.

item.title: 'Hypocritical': Government moves to curb some Iranian visas after footballer asylum cases
item.description: Critics accuse the government of concealing the "true effect" of the changes, arguing they would prevent people from seeking refuge.

item.title: Oil price threatens taxi fare increases and major banks now predict two more rate rises
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Josh Gilbert from eToro to as NAB, Westpac and Citi predict two RBA interest rate rises in a row as oil prices remain high threatening inflation; and Stephanie Youssef speaks with Blair Davies from the Australian Taxi Industry Association to find out, if like some airlines, taxis will need to increase fares to cover the spike in petrol prices.

item.title: Why it's hard to predict just how much airfares will rise amid War in the Middle East
item.description: Qantas has flagged fare increases, citing aviation fuel costs which have soared up to 150 per cent since since the start of the conflict.

item.title: Criticism of Australia's new laws to temporarily ban some people coming into the country
item.description: The federal government has introduced new legislation to impose a temporary ban from some countries travelling to Australia on certain visas. Rights groups and experts have condemned the move, raising concerns that the war in the Middle East could prevent people from seeking asylum in Australia.

item.title: Before offering refuge to Iranian players, Australia took in another team
item.description: Several members of the Iranian football team have chosen asylum in Australia. But it isn't the first time the country has stepped in.

item.title: I'm a scientist. One conversation led me to rethink climate change
item.description: As a teenager growing up in a rural town, Jacob Martin was sceptical about climate change. Some pivotal moments in his life changed that.

item.title: Iran warns of US$200 oil barrels as it retaliates against US-Israeli bombardment
item.description: A spokesperson for Iran's military command warned "the oil price depends on regional security".

item.title: Former ASIO chief says he quit antisemitism royal commission as he felt 'surplus', was 'overpaid'
item.description: Dennis Richardson's sudden departure comes ahead of the royal commission's interim report being delivered next month.

item.title: Iran warns of $US200 a barrel oil, as conflict continues to spread across the Middle East
item.description: The war in the Middle East is continuing to intensify, with Iran warning oil prices could surge to US$200 a barrel if the conflict spreads across the region. As missiles strike Israel and Iran, tensions are rising rise in the Gulf and Lebanon, as fears grow of a wider war and a global economic shock.

item.title: Soldiers deployed in Johannesburg as gang violence and illegal mining grips South African cities
item.description: Soldiers have been deployed to the streets of Johannesburg in the first major troop deployment since South African President Cyril Ramaphosa vowed to crack down on violent crime and illegal mining. With some of the highest crime rates in the world, South Africa is deploying troops to five of its nine provinces in an effort to tackle the growing violence.

item.title: Trump says Iran at the World Cup wouldn't be 'appropriate'. What would happen if they pull out?
item.description: If Iran pulls out of the FIFA World Cup 2026, a replacement may have to be found.

item.title: Two bodies found in Queensland floods are those of missing backpackers, police say
item.description: The first flood-related deaths in the rain-battered region has devastated the community of Gympie, where the bodies were found.

item.title: Petrol prices on rise due to Middle East Conflict | SBS News in Easy English 12 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Government's 100-million-litre fuel promise amid supply 'panic'
item.description: The government has released additional fuel into the domestic market, reassuring Australians that "stocks remain very strong".

item.title: Oil price spikes, Trump’s Iran u-turns and: are you ready player one?
item.description: Trump tries to extinguish the oil price fire but Iran and Israel both double down on an extended war. Iranian women soccer players seek asylum and the Nepali youth’s hip-hop hero. Plus, Ready Player One? The virtual world to the rescue…

item.title: Their journey has just 'begun': Concerns Iranian players monitored by regime 'agents'
item.description: Some are worried about the safety of Iranian players who sought asylum in Australia, raising concerns Iran could keep a close eye on them.

item.title: Fuel standards to be relaxed to boost supply
item.description: Australia's fuel standards will be relaxed for two months to bring more supply into the domestic market. The federal government say the change will add an additional 100 million litres of petrol a month to Australian supply.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Petrol spike adds to taxi industry costs burden – could fares go up?
item.description: The war in Iran has seen high petrol prices translate to higher fuel prices at service stations across the country. The federal government is urging Australian motorists to not panic buy following reports of some stations running out of fuel, while others introduce rations. The effects are being felt in many industries – from airlines and fishers to truck and taxi drivers. Stephanie Youssef spoke with Australian Taxi Industry Association CEO Blair Davies about how the added costs and uncertainty is impacting the already pressured industry

item.title: Matildas v North Korea: Will this Asian Cup quarter-final see Kerr re-enact first goal magic?
item.description: With the fate of two players unknown, will the Matildas be able to secure their spot in the 2027 Women's World Cup?

item.title: AI claims 1,600 jobs at Atlassian & more calls for 2 RBA rate rises
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Tom Gilbertson from Loftus Peak about the future of AI and the workforce and what it means for investors as Atlassian becomes the first major Software as a Service to slash jobs; Trent Sauders from CBA details why the bank is now predicting the RBA will lift interest rates two more times this cycle; and Angus Geddes from Fat Prophets shares his thoughts on the investment environment as the war in the Middle-East continues.

item.title: First people arrested under new Queensland laws banning 'from the river to the sea' chant
item.description: The Queensland government is under fire from activists who consider the hate speech laws to be "hallmarks of an authoritarian police state".

item.title: Buying a unit is cheaper than renting in this inner-city spot
item.description: New research suggests Australians could save hundreds per fortnight by buying rather than renting a unit in certain places.

item.title: Australian officials pulled from Lebanon, Israel and UAE amid war in the Middle East
item.description: Non-essential government staff were told to get out of Israel and the UAE earlier on Thursday.

item.title: Iran vows to keep Strait of Hormuz shut in first statement from new Supreme Leader
item.description: Mojtaba Khamenei says the leverage of closing the Strait of Hormuz should be used and that attacks on Gulf countries will continue.

item.title: Qantas agrees to $105 million settlement over COVID-19 flight credits
item.description: The airlines makes no admission of liability under the terms of the settlement, which still requires Federal Court approval.

item.title: 'Only one winner in this war': How the war on Iran is bankrolling the war on Ukraine
item.description: New data shows that Russia's oil export earnings have soared since the US-Israeli war in Iran broke out, sparking fears that Russia will use this war to finance its own in Ukraine. As the United States lifts sanctions and Europe considers more flexibility, Russia says the world is coming to understand the importance of its resources.

item.title: Iran's Supreme Leader vows revenge as Israel steps up strikes
item.description: Iran’s new Supreme Leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei has vowed retaliation for attacks on Iran as Israeli strikes expand, raising fears the Middle East conflict could widen and further destabilise global energy markets

item.title: INTERVIEW: Bondi hero Ahmed al Ahmed
item.description: It's been almost three months since one of the deadliest mass shootings in Australia's recent history... when 15 people were killed at Sydney's Bondi Beach. 43-year-old Syrian-born Australian tobacco shop owner Ahmed Al- Ahmed tackled and disarmed one of the gunmen, a moment caputred on a video which went viral around the world. He describes the incident as 'painful'. He was shot multiple times while tackling the attacker, undergoing surgery for his injuries. He spoke to SBS Arabic's Dima Alkaed about what happened that day... and his plans moving forward.

item.title: Another conflict is dominating headlines. But what impact does the grim news cycle have on our mental health?
item.description: The recent conflict in Iran and across the Middle East is yet again exposing news audiences to atrocities and human suffering on a large scale. So, what impact does news consumption have on audiences, and how can people stay informed, without becoming distressed?

item.title: Lebanon at 'breaking point', UN warns, as displacement crisis grows
item.description: Lebanon's civilians bear the brunt as fighting spreads, triggering mass displacement and growing international concern.

item.title: $200bn wiped off ASX since start of Middle-East war ahead of RBA meeting
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Morgans Chief Economist Michael Knox who is betting the RBA will need to lift interest rates three times this year as a wave of inflation prepares to come down on the economy; while Stephanie Youssef looks through the day's market action with Zoran Kresovic from Blueberry and how investors are feeling as the Middle-East war rolls on.

item.title: Wyatt Flores and country music as therapy for mental health
item.description: The US country star sees music as an invaluable way to spark conversations about mental health — and give people hope.

item.title: How war in the Middle East is 're-ordering power' and what it means for Australia's alliances
item.description: The war in the Middle East has signalled changes to the international order, with some saying the system is in "severe crisis".

item.title: Amid rationing and shortage fears, here's what Australia's fuel supply looks like in charts
item.description: Energy Minister Chris Bowen has released 20 per cent of the nation's fuel reserves as experts warn rationing could be on the cards.

item.title: How the humble milk bar forever changed Australia
item.description: Milk bars, often run by Greek migrants, forever changed the way Australians meet and eat. Across the country, cafes and milk bars popularised juke boxes, rock and roll and frothy soda drinks.

item.title: Father and son counter religious hatred with 'Cake not Hate' campaign
item.description: Across the UK, hate crimes targeting Muslims have become increasingly prevalent, and are now at record levels, representing almost half of all religious hate crimes in the country. At a local mosque in Peterborough, community members have responded by celebrating a campaign featuring kindness and cupcakes.

item.title: Matildas sink North Korea to secure Women’s Asian Cup semi-final berth
item.description: The 2010 champions will play against either China or Taiwan at Optus Stadium on Tuesday.

item.title: NSW to hold fuel security roundtable as government monitors supply pressures
'A piece of living history': How Greek milk bars changed the way Australians meet and eat
item.description: Australia's historic milk bars, rooted in Greek-Australian culture, keep nostalgia alive with classic treats and timeless charm.

item.title: Kharg Island hit in 'one of the most powerful bombing raids in the history of the Middle East'
item.description: The US military has bombed targets on Iran's Kharg Island, which handles almost all of Iran's crude exports, as US President Donald Trump vowed to hit Iran harder.

item.title: Kharg Island hit in 'one of the most powerful bombing raids in the history of the Middle East'
item.description: The US military has bombed targets on Iran's Kharg Island, which handles almost all of Iran's crude exports, as US President Donald Trump vowed to hit Iran harder.

item.title: Popular tax discount on electric vehicles could be phased out, just as petrol prices rise
item.description: Proponents fear ending the Electric Car Discount could unfairly punish families outside major metropolitan areas battling long commutes.

item.title: Haiti's first Paralympic skier hits the slopes, as Australia's Ben Tudhope wins bronze
item.description: Ralf Etienne, who was trapped under an earthquake in Haiti, wants to win gold in 2030.

item.title: Eleven dead and more than 20 injured in Sudan drone strike
item.description: Drones have become a key ‌weapon in the war between Sudan's army and the Rapid Support Forces that began in April 2023.

item.title: Energy minister urges calm over fuel stocks as consumer watchdog scrutinises price increases
item.description: The government has assured Australians that there is no fuel supply shortage, as the New South Wales government convenes a fuel security roundtable.

item.title: Why Australia's famous flying doctors are more important now than ever before
item.description: "In many communities, the flying doctor isn't just supporting the healthcare system, we are the healthcare system."

item.title: Punters 'unlikely' to see millions in ticket sales refunded as long-running festival shutters
item.description: Bluesfest's founder said it was "unlikely" festivalgoers, some of whom spent thousands on tickets and accommodation, would see any refunds.

item.title: North Korea allegedly fires ballistic missiles during US-Korea drills
item.description: Nuclear-armed North Korea frequently objects to drills by the US and South Korea, calling them "dress rehearsals" for armed aggression.

item.title: Matildas to face China in Asian Cup semi-finals as Taiwan defeated in defensive showdown
item.description: Taiwan played well beyond expectations, keeping China from finding a pair of clear goals until well into stoppage time.

item.title: Matildas to face China in Asian Cup semi-finals as Taiwan defeated in defensive showdown
item.description: Taiwan played well beyond expectations, keeping China from finding a pair of clear goals until well into stoppage time.

item.title: Max lives in a 'renters' utopia', but some say the city's famous housing system isn't fair
item.description: As rental affordability hits a new low in Australia, a European city boasts a decades-long social housing model which houses about 50 per cent of its population.

item.title: For 200 years, this community has called an Australian island home. Now its future is in doubt
item.description: In one of Australia's most remote places, a unique community navigates rising seas and fears of relocation and cultural loss.

item.title: Donald Trump calls for ships to keep the Strait of Hormuz open | Morning Bulletin 15 March 2026
item.description: In today's bulletin, Donald Trump calls on countries to send ships to keep the Strait of Hormuz open; Australia has less than 30 days of jet fuel; and in football, Matildas to face China in Asian Cup semi-finals.

item.title: Fifth member of Iranian football team leaves Australia after withdrawing asylum bid
item.description: Team captain Zahra Ghanbari has withdrawn her asylum bid, after three other members of the team left Australia over the weekend.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Brain health focus from 20s and 30s 'critical' in preventing dementia
item.description: With dementia now confirmed as the leading cause of death in Australia, advocates are calling on the federal government to fund a public health campaign to promote brain health. The CEO of Dementia Australia, professor Tanya Buchanan, speaks to Blake Quinn about what the data is showing and what Australians should be doing to reduce the risk of getting the brain condition.

item.title: Frozen in time: the legacy of the people of Pompeii
item.description: A new permanent exhibition on the ancient Roman city of Pompeii has showcased an arresting image of the moment when a catastrophic volcano eruption destroyed the city in 79 AD. Over 20 casts of bodies of residents in the city will be displayed at the exhibition, along with other findings such as plants and food that remained buried for centuries under metres of ash and lava.

item.title: Trump urges other nations to help secure shipping through Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Iran had threatened that US-linked oil and energy firms would be "turned into a pile of ashes" if they were hit.

item.title: How is watching the war in the Middle East impacting our mental health?
item.description: The recent conflict in Iran and across the Middle East is exposing audiences to atrocities and human suffering on a large scale. Here's how people can stay informed, without becoming overwhelmingly distressed.

item.title: More Iranian women football players to leave Australia | Midday News Bulletin 15 March 2026
item.description: In today's bulletin, Three more members of Iran's women's football team who were granted asylum in Australia return to Iran; St Patrick's Day celebrated with parades, music and dance; and in sport, Formula One races in Bahrain and Saudi Arabia cancelled due to war in the Middle East.

item.title: Danish royals Queen Mary and King Frederik begin Australia visit with Uluru trek
item.description: Denmark's monarchs have followed in the footsteps of the late Princess Diana and now-King Charles with a visit to a site in Australia's red centre.

item.title: Recession not expected in Australia but markets suggest a March rate hike: Treasurer
item.description: Traders and economists widely expect the Reserve Bank to lift interest rates but uncertainty over the Iran war could convince the board to keep them on hold.

item.title: Concern remains for Iran's women's footballers leaving Australia | Evening News Bulletin 15 March 2026
item.description: Concern remains for Iran's women's footballers; The Australian government rejects an informal request from the US for a ship in the Strait of Hormuz; Australia's women's rugby side win - but still lose - at the Seven Series in the US.

item.title: Anti-Muslim hate has been rising. Advocates want to know how the government will respond
item.description: The Australian Human Rights Commission is calling for urgent action to tackle violence, vilification and hate towards Muslims on the International Day to Combat Islamophobia. Race Discrimination Commissioner Giri Sivaraman says he's 'confounded' the government hasn't responded to the National Anti-Racism Framework yet. It was launched in November 2024, but the government is yet to provide a response.

item.title: Sentimental Value just won an Oscar. Meet the skateboard-smuggling director behind it
item.description: Joachim Trier reflects on his family, punk roots, messy lives and the quiet moments that inspire his films.

item.title: Iran denies Trump's claim of seeking ceasefire as it warns countries against wider war
item.description: Countries have responded cautiously to the US president's call to send ships to the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: US says Iran war will be over in a few weeks | Morning News Bulletin 16 March 2026
item.description: The US says the war with Iran will end in a few weeks... New South Wales convenes a roundtable on fuel in the state amid reports of petrol pumps running dry ... and 19-year-old Kimi Antonelli wins the Chinese Grand Prix, after another disaster for Aussie Oscar Piastri

item.title: See the full list: Here are the winners of the 2026 Oscars
item.description: One Battle After Another swept the ceremony with six awards, including best picture, with Sinners close behind.

item.title: See the full list: Here are the winners of the 2026 Oscars
item.description: One Battle After Another swept the ceremony with six awards, including best picture, with Sinners close behind.

item.title: Energy Minister says Australia’s fuel supply remains secure | Midday News Bulletin 16 March 2026
item.description: Energy Minister Chris Bowen says Australia’s fuel supply remains secure, Warnings for young Australians turning to social media for financial advice, McLaren launches investigation into Piastri's technical failure.

item.title: US claims conflict with Iran will end in the next few weeks, as oil concerns pile up
item.description: The United States says its conflict with Iran will end in the next few weeks. But not all indicators are pointing to that outcome, as economic concerns continue to dominate around the world, alongside the military strikes.

item.title: Iran warns countries not to get involved in war | Easy English 16 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Fresh warning over risky financial advice targeting young Australians
item.description: A survey commissioned by the country's financial watchdog suggests nearly two thirds of gen Z trust financial information on social media.

item.title: How to make SBS News your preferred source on Google
item.description: Google now allows you to pick your preferred sources in Top Stories.

item.title: UN chief condemns cycles of war with 850,000 people uprooted in Lebanon
item.description: A displacement crisis has accelerated in Lebanon over the last fortnight, driven by the widening war in the Middle East. Nearly 15 per cent of Lebanon's population have been forced to flee their homes in the past fortnight and the UN is warning the humanitarian situation is set to further deteriorate.

item.title: Iran denies it asked the US for a ceasefire | Evening News Bulletin 16 March 2026
item.description: Iran denies it asked the United States for a ceasefire, Victorian government has settled a COVID 19 class action, Danish cyclist Jonas Vingegaard wins the Paris-Nice race.

item.title: Questions raised over Australia's long-term energy security
item.description: The Federal Government has authorised a temporary 60-day easing of sulphur standards to inject high-sulphur petrol into regional communities facing critical fuel shortages. While Energy Minister Chris Bowen maintains that national reserves are stable, critics and analysts warn that Australia’s decade-long failure to meet international stockpile obligations leaves the country vulnerable to prolonged geopolitical shocks.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Will the war in the Middle East cause the RBA to raise interest rates?
item.description: The war in the Middle East has rocked the financial world, with the price of oil sky rocketing and no end to the conflict in sight. This week will see the Reserve Bank set the interest rate - and the markets are now predicting there's likely to be a hike in the rates. Cameron Carr spoke to the AMP's Chief Economist Shane Oliver. He says it's an uncertain situation.

item.title: Does a 'Great Firewall' loom? Why Russians are buying up walkie-talkies and road maps
item.description: Some believe Russia is testing a system that could cut it off from the global internet.

item.title: 'One Battle After Another' also wins Oscars battle
item.description: One Battle After Another has bested Sinners at this year's Oscars, winning six awards to Sinners' four. KPop Demon Hunters and Frankenstein were among the other winners at Hollywood's night of nights. Michael B. Jordan and Jessie Buckley won the top acting gongs.

item.title: Markets brace for rate rise and can you trust AI for financial advice?
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Mathan Somasundaram from Deep Data Analytics and looks ahead to tomorrow's RBA interest rate decision on what will be a big week for global central banks; plus Stephanie Youssef looks at how Generation Z is seeking out financial advice with ASIC's Adam Kirkland.

item.title: Trump criticises allies for resisting call to help clear Strait of Hormuz | Morning Bulletin 17 March 2026
item.description: Donald Trump criticises allies for resisting his call to send ships to the Strait of Hormuz; stronger regulation announced for migration agents; and in sport, the Sydney Swans apologise to the Jewish community over a tribute to victims of the Bondi attack.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Race Discrimination Commissioner Giridharan Sivaraman
item.description: In a week that began with the International Day to Combat Islamophobia, SBS's Tee Mitchell spoke to Australia's Race Discrimination Commissioner at the Human Rights Commission, Giridharan Sivaraman, about Islamophobia in this country and race relations in general.

item.title: Justin says he was a jealous boyfriend. Then he found a 'long-term fix'
item.description: "Constant reassurance" was a short-term fix for Justin's jealousy. It took therapy, time and meditation to find the "long-term" one.

item.title: The RBA faces its 'most complicated' rates call in years. What could it mean for you?
item.description: The Reserve Bank was until recently expected to keep rates on hold at today's meeting. Now economists aren't so sure.

item.title: Israeli ground offensive in Lebanon 'must be averted', countries say in joint statement
item.description: Leaders of Canada, France, Germany, Italy and the UK have warned of "devastating consequences" as Israel says it's conducting a ground operation.

item.title: Australia not among US allies asked to send navy vessels to the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Defence Minister Richard Marles says the government would consider any request to participate in the conflict, but none had yet arrived.

item.title: Consumer watchdog summoning major fuel suppliers to explain skyrocketing prices
item.description: Labor has previously asked the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission to monitor prices after war erupted to ensure motorists weren't taken for "mugs".

item.title: Reserve Bank to hand down decision on interest rates| Midday News Bulletin 17 March 2026
item.description: Reserve Board to hand down decision on interest rates, A national power outage in Cuba, Australian basketball great Ken Cole dies aged 82

item.title: Remaining Iranian footballers join A-League team for training session as teammates depart
item.description: Five of the seven squad members who sought asylum in Australia and reversed their decisions have rejoined the team in Kuala Lumpur.

item.title: Allies resist President Trump's calls to send warships to Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Global oil prices have increased by 40 to 50 percent as Iran attacks shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. President Donald Trump says the United States is negotiating with countries heavily reliant on Middle East oil, after he demanded several nations send warships to re-open the Strait of Hormuz. But European allies have so far proved resistant to President Trump's request.

item.title: 'We don't want to lose our town': Woolworths' move strikes fear among residents
item.description: The closure of Rio Tinto's bauxite mine will be tough enough for this Arnhem Land town, but now Woolworths may pull out too.

item.title: Taiwanese Indigenous dance at Women's Asian Cup briefly cancelled, politician claims
item.description: A Taiwanese Indigenous dance performance was reinstated before the Taiwan-China football quarter final on Saturday, after diplomats intervened.

item.title: Changes to migration agent rules | SBS News in Easy English 17 March 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Australians in Lebanon face $17k travel nightmare as daughter needs medical care
item.description: The family had planned to come back to Australia at the end of April, but are now trying to return more urgently.

item.title: Has Vienna solved the problem on housing affordability?
item.description: Rental affordability has hit a new low in Australia - but a European city boasts a century-old social housing model, which houses about 50 per cent of its population. With low rental costs and rolling leases, it's led some to refer to it as a 'renter's utopia'.

item.title: The Reserve Bank raises interest rates to 4.1 per cent | Evening News Bulletin 17 March 2026
item.description: The Reserve Bank raises interest rates to 4.1 per cent, Indigenous leaders are urging the government to support vital services in a remote Northern Territory town, The Asian Football Confederation says it's confident Iran will compete at this year's Men's FIFA World Cup.

item.title: 'My kids are scared': Melbourne mother stranded in Lebanon pleads for return to Australia
item.description: A Melbourne mother stranded in Lebanon with her children is pleading for help to return to Australia as war rages around them. Iman Nayef travelled to southern Lebanon to visit relatives, arriving just one day before the conflict began. Now trapped as Israeli strikes and Hezbollah rocket fire intensify, she says her children are terrified as explosions echo non-stop.

item.title: Despite a rate rise, Australia's CPI will likely remain among highest in the world
item.description: Australia's consumer price index of 3.8 per cent is one of the highest in the world.

item.title: Interest rates go up: decision reflects growing uncertainty in the global economy
item.description: The Reserve Bank has hiked interest rates by 25 basis points to 4.1 per cent. It's the second rise in a row - and there could be more to come.

item.title: Afghanistan says 400 killed in Kabul hospital attack as Pakistan denies responsibility
item.description: Afghan authorities allege a drug-user rehabilitation hospital was hit amid an escalating conflict with its neighbour, Pakistan.

item.title: Matildas to take on nine-time Asian Cup winners China, with a familiar face on the team
item.description: Matildas head coach Joe Montemurro says tonight's game will be a "tactical battle".

item.title: RBA raises interest rates in March for second time in 2026
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Nick Stenner from Bank of America; Alice Shen from VanEck; and Sally Tindall from Canstar to find out why the RBA lifted interest rates, how the market reacted and what it means for borrowers.

item.title: Government reverses SBS Western Sydney expansion decision
item.description: The government says it will continue to back the "important work" of SBS after a decision the broadcaster described as disappointing.

item.title: Sam Kerr’s moment of magic sends Matildas into Women’s Asian Cup final with 2-1 win over China
item.description: The Matildas will now face Japan or South Korea in Saturday’s decider in Sydney as they chase their first Asian Cup title since 2010.

item.title: Sam Kerr’s moment of magic sends Matildas into Women’s Asian Cup final with 2-1 win over China
item.description: The Matildas will now face Japan or South Korea in Saturday’s decider in Sydney as they chase their first Asian Cup title since 2010.

item.title: US claims oil tankers beginning to cross Strait of Hormuz
item.description: White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett called the movement "a sign of how little Iran has left."

item.title: The 90-day question looming over Australia's fuel price pain
item.description: Global oil disruption has put Australia's fuel security back in focus.

item.title: Israel kills Iranian security chief Larijani | Morning News Bulletin 18 March 2026
item.description: In this bulletin, Israel says it has killed a top Iranian security chief; A nervous wait for flood zones as a tropical cyclone looms off the Queensland coast; and in sport, The Matildas have defeated defending champions China to go through to the Asian Cup final.

item.title: 'Cashing in big time': Who's profiting from the war in the Middle East?
item.description: War in the Middle East has left hundreds of people dead and left destruction in its wake — and there's also an "uncomfortable truth".

item.title: Iran vows revenge after Israel kills security chief; new supreme leader rejects ceasefire
item.description: An Iranian army chief has vowed to launch a "decisive and regrettable" retaliation.

item.title: Iran vows revenge after Israel kills security chief; new supreme leader rejects ceasefire
item.description: An Iranian army chief has vowed to launch a "decisive and regrettable" retaliation.

item.title: Trump says he doesn't 'need or desire' Australia's help in outburst over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Trump said his NATO allies were making a "very foolish mistake" and lumped Australia in on the list of countries he's unhappy with.

item.title: National cabinet to meet over fuel shortage concerns | Midday News Bulletin 18 March 2026
item.description: Iran confirms the death of powerful national security chief in an Israeli air strike; New figures show domestic violence offences at a record high in New South Wales courts; And in sport, Senegal has been stripped of the Africa Cup of Nations title it won in January.

item.title: Tropical Cyclone Narelle now expected to cross far north Queensland coast as Category 4 system
item.description: The tropical cyclone is expected to arrive on the coast between Cooktown and Lockhart River early on Thursday.

item.title: UN rights office says Israeli strikes on Lebanese civilians may be war crimes
item.description: The UN human rights office has called for an investigation into deadly strikes on displaced people sheltering in tents.

item.title: Zelenskyy, Starmer urge continuing support for Ukraine as world's attention turns to Iran
item.description: The war in Ukraine is intersecting with rising instability in the Middle East and pressure on global energy supplies. President Volodymyr Zelenskyy is in London, warning modern warfare is rapidly evolving and pushing for deeper defence cooperation. At the same time, the EU is moving to resolve an oil dispute threatening support for Kyiv.

item.title: SBS News In Easy English 18 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5 minute news bulletin for English learners and people with a disability.

item.title: Top US counterterrorism official resigns over Iran war
item.description: A top U-S counterterrorism official has resigned over the war in the Middle East. Meanwhile, Donald Trump has lashed out at NATO allies and Australia, saying the U-S no longer needs their assistance. And Israel's military says it'll continue to target Iranian leaders.

item.title: Four-day week, jackets off: The world starts cutting back on fuel amid supply concerns
item.description: As parts of Asia adopt fuel rationing measures, the Australian government insists it's not on the cards.

item.title: Iranian missile strikes Australian base | Evening News Bulletin March 18 2026
item.description: In this bulletin ... ** An Australian military base in the Middle East struck by an Iranian missile.... ** The United Nations says some Israeli airstrikes on civilians in Lebanon may amount to war crimes. ** And in sport, Real Madrid beats Manchester City in the UEFA Champions League...

item.title: 'A real crossroads': Donald Trump, his 'great test', and the fine line walked by US allies
item.description: Trump's latest outburst exposes the tightrope American allies are balancing on amid the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Government ditches plan for SBS hub in multicultural Western Sydney
item.description: Plans to build a production hub for the Special Broadcasting Service in Western Sydney will no longer go ahead. The government has indicated its decision has been made in the context of the current fiscal environment.

item.title: New mining CEOs, plus markets brace for US rates decision
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Jamie Hannah from VanEck about the day's market action including the latest global interest rate expectations and what it means for investments, while BHP announces who will replace CEO Mike Henry.

item.title: Government modelling 'more drastic' fuel scenario, Chalmers says, as war rages on
item.description: Under one scenario, oil would reach US$120 a barrel and take three years to return to its pre-war price.

item.title: 'Big mistake': Why President Trump's plan to 'take Cuba' may be a major miscalculation
item.description: As the humanitarian and energy crisis in Cuba worsens, US President Donald Trump has announced he plans on "taking" the Caribbean island. Desperate for relief from a US-imposed blockade, Cuban citizens and members of the diaspora say that while they're hopeful for change, they're fearful of the US agenda.
